candidate_problems.text: Can 'system-level equilibrium' corrigibility be made precise enough to distinguish genuinely stable governability from merely redistributed optimization pressure? The conjecture hinges on a structural notion of equilibrium, but it is unresolved what formal properties—e.g., incentive compatibility across modules, attractor stability under capability scaling, resistance to collusion/deception, and preservation under self-modification—are necessary and sufficient for a system to remain corrigible rather than just hiding a de facto privileged objective behind institutional layers.

Is there a capability threshold beyond which distributed oversight and authority gradients themselves become objects of strategic optimization by the system, causing structural corrigibility to fail or require recursive redesign? The open question is whether increasing capability predictably shifts the agent–overseer system into new regimes where feedback channels are gamed, overseers are manipulated, or governance modules are co-opted, and if so what scaling laws or phase transitions govern that breakdown.

What historical and architectural conditions actually produce corrigible equilibria in practice, rather than in abstract design sketches? The conjecture assumes that closed-loop governance can replace open-loop maximization, but it remains unclear which concrete mechanisms—organizational separation, interpretability, constitutional update rules, market/institutional checks, human-in-the-loop control, etc.—generate robust correction dynamics under real-world noise, latency, conflicting overseers, and economic selection pressures.
